editor_settings.title: Canon G7X: the compact camera that outlived its era
editor_settings.content:
J’ai enfin rentabilisé mon canon g7x pic.twitter.com/u5GdvvlEpl

, Princesse (@Kingsarah_era) April 7, 2026

The Canon G7X series wasn’t supposed to become a cult object. When Canon released the original G7X back in 2014, it was simply a response to Sony’s RX100 line, a premium compact with a larger sensor and a bright lens. On paper, it made sense. In reality, it ended up doing something unexpected: it became the camera people kept coming back to, even as smartphones got better and mirrorless cameras got cheaper.

Fast forward to now, and the G7X, especially the Mark II and Mark III, exists in a strange space. It’s outdated in specs, overhyped on social media, hard to find, and still… weirdly relevant.

J’ai enfin rentabilisé mon canon g7x pic.twitter.com/u5GdvvlEpl

, Princesse (@Kingsarah_era) April 7, 2026


The hardware: small body, serious intent

Strip away the hype and the G7X is still built around a very specific idea: portability without sacrificing image quality.

At its core, you’re getting:

  • A 1-inch sensor (much larger than a phone sensor)
  • Around 20 megapixels of resolution
  • A 24–100mm equivalent lens with a fast f/1.8–2.8 aperture

That lens is the real story. It’s bright enough for low light, flexible enough for everyday shooting, and paired with built-in stabilization that makes handheld shooting forgiving. You don’t think about settings too much, you just shoot.

The Mark II added Canon’s DIGIC 7 processor, improving autofocus, noise handling, and general responsiveness . It also introduced small but meaningful usability upgrades: a better grip, a tilting touchscreen, and faster burst shooting.

None of this is groundbreaking today. But the balance still feels right in your hands, something many newer cameras overcomplicate.

[product-card:canon-powershot-g7-x-mark-iii-digital-camera-201mp-4k-point-and-shoot-camera-black]

Why people still want it (and sometimes overpay for it)

Here’s where things get interesting.

The G7X didn’t stay relevant because of specs. It stayed relevant because of aesthetic and experience.

On platforms like TikTok, the camera has become shorthand for a specific look:

  • direct flash photos
  • slightly soft contrast
  • “early 2000s digital” vibe
  • flattering skin tones straight out of camera

That last point matters more than people admit. Canon’s color science has always leaned warm and pleasing, and the G7X delivers images that often don’t need editing.

One Reddit user summed it up pretty bluntly:

“People want that airbrushed flash look… and portability.”

It’s not about technical perfection. It’s about feel.
